title: Venus – Jupiter “Cosmic Kiss” Conjunction
shortDescription: Venus and Jupiter will appear extremely close together in the evening sky, creating a brilliant pair brighter than any star.
mainDescription: On June 9, 2026, Venus and Jupiter will come within about 1.6 degrees of each other — roughly the width of a little finger held at arm’s length. Although they are separated by hundreds of millions of kilometers in space, they will look almost like a single dazzling object. This striking alignment features the two brightest planets in the night sky. The pair will be visible low in the western sky shortly after sunset. Venus will shine much brighter than Jupiter, making the scene particularly dramatic. Such close and bright conjunctions of these two planets are rare and memorable.
whatYoullSee: A bright double “star” low in the evening sky, shining far brighter than anything else around it.
whyItMatters: This is one of the most visually impressive and easily observable planetary alignments visible to the naked eye.
keyFacts: Two brightest planets in the sky
Visible to the naked eye even from cities
nextOccurrences: Next bright Venus-Jupiter conjunctions are expected in the early 2030s. Similar spectacular pairings occur roughly every 10–13 years.

title: Greatest Solar Eclipse (Europe)
shortDescription: A total solar eclipse will cross parts of Europe, bringing up to 2 minutes and 18 seconds of totality.
mainDescription: On August 12, 2026, a total solar eclipse will pass over Iceland, northern Spain, and parts of Portugal — the first total solar eclipse visible from mainland Europe since 1999. The path of totality will also cross Greenland and the Arctic region. In the best locations, the Moon will completely cover the Sun for up to 2 minutes and 18 seconds. Observers will experience sudden daytime darkness, a bright solar corona, and stars becoming visible. This event is one of the most accessible and impressive eclipses of the decade for European skywatchers.
whatYoullSee: The Sun fully covered by the Moon, a glowing white corona, sudden twilight in the middle of the day, and bright stars or planets in the darkened sky.
whyItMatters: It is one of the best total solar eclipses visible from Europe in many years and offers an excellent opportunity for both public viewing and scientific study.
keyFacts: Path crosses Iceland, northern Spain, and Portugal
First total solar eclipse in mainland Europe since 1999
nextOccurrences: Next major total solar eclipse with good European visibility: September 3, 2081
Other total solar eclipses will occur in different parts of the world in the coming decades.

title: 99942 Apophis Close Flyby
shortDescription: Asteroid Apophis will pass extremely close to Earth — closer than many satellites — and may be visible to the naked eye as a moving star.
mainDescription: On April 13, 2029, the asteroid 99942 Apophis will make one of the closest known flybys of a large asteroid in modern history. It will pass approximately 31,600 km (19,600 miles) from Earth’s surface — closer than geostationary satellites. Once considered a potential impact threat, it is now known to be completely safe. At its closest, Apophis will reach magnitude 3.1 and move noticeably across the sky. This rare event offers scientists a unique opportunity to study how Earth’s gravity affects an asteroid’s rotation and orbit.
whatYoullSee: A fast-moving point of light crossing the night sky, visible to the naked eye under dark skies as a star-like object that shifts position over minutes.
whyItMatters: It is one of the closest approaches of a sizable asteroid in recorded history and a major event for both astronomy and planetary defense studies.
keyFacts: Closest approach: ~31,600 km from Earth’s surface
Visible to the naked eye in dark skies
Best viewed from Europe, Africa, and western Asia
nextOccurrences: Apophis will not make another close approach like this for over 100 years. The next significant close pass is expected in 2060, but much farther away.

title: Taurid Meteor Swarm (Fireball Years)
shortDescription: Earth will pass through a dense part of the Taurid meteor stream, producing many bright fireballs.
mainDescription: In October 2032, Earth will encounter a particularly rich portion of the Taurid meteor complex. Astronomers expect a significant increase in bright fireballs and slow-moving meteors. Unlike typical meteor showers, the Taurids are known for producing exceptionally bright and long-lasting fireballs. The enhanced activity may last for several weeks. This event is sometimes called the “Fireball Years” due to the higher chance of seeing spectacular meteors. Dark skies away from city lights will offer the best viewing.
whatYoullSee: Slow, bright fireballs streaking across the sky, some lasting several seconds and leaving glowing trains.
whyItMatters: This is one of the best opportunities in the 2030s to see numerous bright fireballs from a single meteor complex.
keyFacts: Meteors are slow but often very bright
Best observed after midnight in dark skies
nextOccurrences: The next strong Taurid fireball years are expected around 2040 and then again in the 2050s.
Normal Taurid activity occurs every year in October and November, but 2032 is predicted to be significantly stronger.

title: Blood Moon Tetrad
shortDescription: Four total lunar eclipses (Blood Moons) will occur in a row over 18 months — a rare lunar tetrad.
mainDescription: Between April 2032 and October 2033, four consecutive total lunar eclipses will take place. Each eclipse will turn the Moon a deep reddish color as it passes completely through Earth’s shadow. This sequence is known as a lunar tetrad. Tetrads are relatively rare, happening only a few times per century. All four eclipses will be visible from large parts of the world, making them accessible to millions of people. Lunar eclipses are safe to observe with the naked eye and create stunning photographic opportunities.
whatYoullSee: The Moon gradually darkening and turning copper-red during totality, lasting up to nearly two hours per eclipse.
whyItMatters: A Blood Moon Tetrad is a striking and memorable series that captures public attention and provides excellent opportunities for skywatchers.
keyFacts: Four total lunar eclipses in 18 months
Visible from large portions of Earth
Safe to watch with the naked eye
nextOccurrences: The next lunar tetrad after 2032–2033 will occur in 2043–2044.
Lunar tetrads happen only a few times every century.

title: Great Planetary Alignment
shortDescription: Several bright planets will line up across the sky in a spectacular planetary parade.
mainDescription: In September 2034, a striking alignment of multiple bright planets will be visible in the evening or morning sky. Mercury, Venus, Mars, Jupiter, and Saturn may all appear together along the ecliptic. Such planetary alignments, often called “parades of planets,” create a beautiful and memorable sight. The planets will stretch across a large portion of the sky, making it easy to observe with the naked eye. This event will attract attention from both casual skywatchers and astronomy enthusiasts.
whatYoullSee: Five or more bright planets visible at once, lined up like beads on a string across the sky.
whyItMatters: Great planetary alignments are visually impressive and generate widespread public interest in astronomy.
keyFacts: Best observed from dark or semi-dark locations
Occurs roughly every 10–20 years for strong alignments
nextOccurrences: The next strong planetary alignment is expected around 2040.
Smaller alignments of 3–4 planets occur more frequently, every few years.

title: Great Mars Opposition
shortDescription: Mars will reach its closest approach to Earth, appearing exceptionally bright and large in the night sky.
mainDescription: In September 2035, Mars will come to one of its best oppositions of the coming decades. At its closest, the planet will be only about 56 million kilometers from Earth. This makes Mars significantly brighter and larger than usual, reaching a magnitude of -2.8. Observers will be able to see its orange-red color clearly with the naked eye and details on its surface through small telescopes. This is one of the most favorable oppositions for amateur astronomers in the next 20 years.
whatYoullSee: A very bright orange-red “star” that outshines most other planets, with visible surface details in a telescope.
whyItMatters: Close Mars oppositions like this offer excellent opportunities for both casual viewing and serious astronomical observation.
nextOccurrences: The next similarly close Mars opposition will occur in 2041.
Mars reaches opposition roughly every 26 months, but truly great ones happen less frequently.

title: Extreme Supermoon
shortDescription: One of the closest and brightest full Moons of the decade, appearing significantly larger and brighter than average.
mainDescription: In November 2037, the Moon will reach its closest point to Earth (perigee) at nearly the same time as the full Moon phase. This combination creates an Extreme Supermoon. The Moon will appear up to 14% larger and 30% brighter than a typical full Moon. The difference is especially noticeable when the Moon is low on the horizon. While supermoons occur several times per year, this particular one stands out due to its exceptional proximity.
whatYoullSee: A noticeably larger and brighter full Moon, especially impressive when rising or setting near the horizon.
whyItMatters: Extreme supermoons create beautiful photo opportunities and help remind people to look up at the night sky.
keyFacts: One of the closest full Moons of the decade
Up to 14% larger and 30% brighter than average
Occurs when perigee and full Moon coincide closely
nextOccurrences: Extreme supermoons occur every few years when the Moon’s perigee is particularly close.
Regular supermoons happen several times each year.

title: Saturn Ring Plane Crossing
shortDescription: Saturn’s famous rings will appear almost invisible as they align edge-on with Earth.
mainDescription: On October 15, 2038, Saturn’s rings will be oriented exactly edge-on from our perspective on Earth. During this brief period, the wide and spectacular rings will seem to disappear almost completely, leaving Saturn looking like a simple golden disk with a thin line. This phenomenon occurs roughly every 15 years. It provides a unique opportunity to observe Saturn’s moons more clearly and study the ring system itself. After the crossing, the rings will gradually open up again and become brighter over the following years.
whatYoullSee: Saturn appearing as a plain oval disk with almost no visible rings — a rare and striking change from its usual appearance.
whyItMatters: This is one of the most interesting viewing angles of Saturn and helps astronomers study its ring dynamics.
keyFacts: Rings appear nearly invisible for several weeks
nextOccurrences: The next ring plane crossing after 2038 will occur around 2053.
This event repeats roughly every 15 years due to Saturn’s orbital tilt.

title: Greatest Solar Eclipse (North America)
shortDescription: One of the greatest total solar eclipses of the 21st century will cross the United States and Canada.
mainDescription: On August 12, 2045, a major total solar eclipse will sweep across North America. This eclipse stands out due to its long duration of totality and excellent path across densely populated regions. In the best locations, the Moon will completely cover the Sun for up to 6 minutes and 6 seconds. The path of totality will pass through California, Nevada, Utah, Colorado, Kansas, Missouri, Illinois, Indiana, Ohio, Pennsylvania, New York, and parts of Canada. Millions of people will be able to witness this spectacular event without traveling far.
whatYoullSee: Complete darkness during the day, a brilliant solar corona, bright planets and stars visible in the sky, and a dramatic twilight on the horizon.
whyItMatters: This is one of the longest and most accessible total solar eclipses in North America for decades.
keyFacts: Path crosses the entire United States from west to east
Visible to tens of millions of people
nextOccurrences: The next total solar eclipse with similar long duration across North America is not expected until the 2070s.
Other total solar eclipses will occur in different parts of the world in the coming decades.

title: Brightest Jupiter of the Century
shortDescription: Jupiter will reach its closest opposition of the century, appearing exceptionally bright and large.
mainDescription: On September 21, 2056, Jupiter will come to its closest approach to Earth in the entire 21st century. The planet will shine at magnitude -2.9, making it brighter than any star in the night sky. At this distance, Jupiter’s disk will appear significantly larger, allowing excellent views of its cloud bands, the Great Red Spot, and its four largest moons through even small telescopes. This will be the best opportunity to observe Jupiter for many decades.
whatYoullSee: An incredibly bright planet with visible cloud bands and moons when viewed through a telescope.
whyItMatters: This is the best viewing conditions for Jupiter in the entire century.
keyFacts: Closest Jupiter opposition of the 21st century
Excellent for both naked-eye and telescopic observation
nextOccurrences: Jupiter reaches opposition every 13 months, but this is the closest and brightest one of the century.
The next very close opposition will occur much later in the 22nd century.

title: Halley’s Comet Return
shortDescription: The most famous comet in history will return to the inner Solar System after 76 years.
mainDescription: In July 2061, Halley’s Comet will make its next return to the inner Solar System. This iconic comet, visible since ancient times, will develop a bright coma and a long tail as it approaches the Sun. Although its brightness is difficult to predict precisely, many astronomers expect it to become visible to the naked eye for several weeks. This will be the first return of Halley’s Comet since 1986, giving a new generation the chance to see one of the most historic objects in the night sky.
whatYoullSee: A bright comet with a glowing head and a long tail stretching across the night sky under dark conditions.
whyItMatters: Halley’s Comet is the most famous periodic comet and connects modern observers with centuries of astronomical history.
keyFacts: May become visible to the naked eye
nextOccurrences: Halley’s Comet will return again in 2134.
After 2134, the next return is expected in 2209.

title: Greatest Solar Eclipse (Europe)
shortDescription: A major total solar eclipse will cross much of Europe with long duration of totality.
mainDescription: On September 3, 2081, a significant total solar eclipse will sweep across Europe. This event is expected to be one of the best total solar eclipses visible from the continent in the second half of the 21st century. The path of totality will pass through several densely populated countries, offering millions of people the chance to witness the phenomenon. In the best locations, the Moon will completely cover the Sun for more than 5 minutes. Observers will experience sudden daytime darkness and a spectacular view of the solar corona.
whatYoullSee: The Sun fully hidden by the Moon, a bright glowing corona, stars visible during the day, and dramatic twilight on the horizon.
whyItMatters: This will be one of the most accessible and longest total solar eclipses in Europe for many decades.
keyFacts: Long duration of totality (over 5 minutes in best locations)
nextOccurrences: The next total solar eclipse with good visibility across Europe is not expected until the 22nd century.
Other total solar eclipses will continue to occur in different parts of the world.

title: Uranus–Neptune Conjunction
shortDescription: Uranus and Neptune will appear very close together in the sky — a rare conjunction of the two ice giants.
mainDescription: In July 2093, Uranus and Neptune will reach a rare conjunction, appearing only about 1 degree apart in the night sky. This is an exceptionally rare event because these two distant planets move very slowly and meet only once every 172 years. Although both planets are too faint to see with the naked eye, they will be easily visible together through binoculars or a small telescope. This alignment provides a unique opportunity for astronomers to observe both ice giants in the same field of view.
whatYoullSee: Two faint blue-green planets very close to each other when viewed through binoculars or a telescope.
whyItMatters: Such conjunctions between Uranus and Neptune occur only once every 172 years, making it a truly once-in-a-lifetime event for most people.
keyFacts: Visible with binoculars or small telescope
Both planets will be in the constellation Virgo
nextOccurrences: The next Uranus–Neptune conjunction will occur in 2265 (172 years later).

title: Transit of Venus
shortDescription: Venus will pass directly across the face of the Sun, appearing as a small black dot.
mainDescription: On December 10, 2117, Venus will transit across the Sun — one of the rarest predictable astronomical events. During the transit, Venus will appear as a small black disk slowly moving across the bright surface of the Sun. Transits of Venus occur in pairs separated by more than a century, making them extremely uncommon. The last pair occurred in 2004 and 2012. This event will be visible from parts of the world and will require proper solar filters for safe observation. Historically, Venus transits helped scientists measure the distance between the Earth and the Sun.
whatYoullSee: A tiny black dot (Venus) slowly crossing the disk of the Sun over several hours.
whyItMatters: This is one of the rarest celestial events that can be predicted far in advance.
keyFacts: Occurs in pairs separated by over 100 years
Last transit pair was in 2004 and 2012
Requires solar filter or safe projection method
nextOccurrences: The second transit of the pair will occur on December 8, 2125.
After 2125, the next transit of Venus will not occur until 2247.

title: Second Transit of Venus
shortDescription: The second and final transit of Venus in the current pair, occurring just 8 years after the first.
mainDescription: On December 8, 2125, Venus will once again pass directly across the face of the Sun, completing the rare pair of transits that began in 2117. Venus will appear as a small black dot slowly crossing the Sun’s disk over several hours. These paired transits are extremely rare — they occur only once every 105 to 121 years. After this event, no one alive will see another transit of Venus for more than a century. This will be the last opportunity for many generations to witness this historic astronomical phenomenon.
whatYoullSee: A tiny black dot (Venus) slowly moving across the bright surface of the Sun.
whyItMatters: This completes one of the rarest predictable celestial events, closing a pair that will not repeat for over 100 years.
keyFacts: Occurs 8 years after the first transit
Requires solar filter for safe viewing
nextOccurrences: After 2125, the next transit of Venus will not occur until December 2247.

title: Annular Solar Eclipse (Ring of Fire)
shortDescription: The Moon will pass in front of the Sun, creating a spectacular “ring of fire” effect.
mainDescription: On February 6, 2126, an annular solar eclipse will occur as the Moon passes between Earth and the Sun. Because the Moon will be slightly farther away, it will not completely cover the Sun, leaving a bright ring of sunlight around its edges. This creates the famous “Ring of Fire” appearance. The eclipse will be visible across parts of the Pacific, North America, and the Atlantic. While the sky will not go completely dark, the visual effect is dramatic and beautiful.
whatYoullSee: A bright ring of sunlight surrounding the dark silhouette of the Moon.
whyItMatters: Annular eclipses produce one of the most visually unique and photogenic effects in astronomy.
keyFacts: Moon appears smaller than the Sun
Path crosses the Pacific and parts of North America
nextOccurrences: Annular solar eclipses occur more frequently than total ones, usually every few years somewhere on Earth.

title: Comet Swift–Tuttle Return
shortDescription: The parent comet of the Perseid meteor shower will return to the inner Solar System.
mainDescription: In August 2126, Comet Swift–Tuttle will make its return to the inner Solar System after 132 years. This large comet is responsible for the annual Perseid meteor shower every August. As it approaches the Sun, it may develop a bright coma and a noticeable tail. While its brightness is difficult to predict decades in advance, many comets of this size become visible to the naked eye under dark skies. This event will give future observers a rare chance to see the actual source of one of the most popular meteor showers.
whatYoullSee: A comet with a glowing head and possibly a long tail, visible in dark skies.
whyItMatters: It connects the famous Perseid meteor shower with its parent comet, offering a direct link between annual meteors and their origin.
keyFacts: Parent body of the Perseid meteor shower
May become visible to the naked eye
nextOccurrences: Comet Swift–Tuttle will return again around 2259.
